EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An experienced snowmobiler got caught in an avalanche in Utah's Uinta Mountains. He was buried by the snow and died at the scene.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two experienced snowmobilers from Evanston, Wyoming were riding in the Humpy Creek drainage in the western Uinta Mountains. Since they did not plan to ride on steep terrain, they left their avalanche beacons and shovels in their vehicles. One rider, a 54 year old man, apparently decided to at least partially ascend a popular snowmobile slope called Cherry Hill, where he triggered an avalanche and was buried. By the time his friend was able to get help, he had been buried under two feet of snow for more than half an hour, and he died at the scene.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into the Great Basin Region pulling a strong cold front with it. The front stalled over northeast Utah, where it produced heavy snowfall over the eastern Utah mountains. The front then pushed through western Colorado spreading heavy snow over the Colorado mountains and higher valleys across northwest Colorado. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

Extensive blowing and drifting snow was generated by frequent wind gusts to 55 mph, with locally stronger gusts up to 68 mph on top of La Sal Mountain.  This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As a broad and deep trough of low pressure moved onto the eastern plains, moisture wrapping around the system spread another round of mountain snow across northeast Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong winter storm system moved into the Great Basin Region pulling a strong cold front with it. The front stalled over northeast Utah, where it produced heavy snowfall over the eastern Utah mountains. The front then pushed through western Colorado spreading heavy snow over the Colorado mountains and higher valleys across northwest Colorado. This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snow produced accumulations of 12 to 15 inches above 8,000 feet, with 6 to 12 inches elsewhere. Winds gusted to 40 mph generating areas of blowing snow.  This event began in March, so please see the March 2010 Storm Data Publication for additional information.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong arctic cold front moved through the state of Utah a couple days before Thanksgiving. This front brought blizzard conditions to northern Utah and very cold air to the entire state.

Storm totals included 8 inches of new snow in West Jordan, 7 inches in Stansbury Park, and 5 inches in the Upper Avenues. UDOT studies showed that commuters paid heed to the warnings, as peak PM hour traffic was approximately 58% less than historic volume, while pre-peak shoulder traffic was higher. UDOT also  calculated that 50,000 hours of travel time was saved by people leaving for the|commute early, equating to a savings of $1,000,000.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A skier was caught, carried, and killed by an avalanche in Hell's Canyon, in the |Snowbasin backcountry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Following several days of fresh snowfall, a skier triggered an avalanche just out-of-bounds of the Snowbasin resort, in the area known as Hell's Canyon. He was caught, carried, and partially buried by the avalanche. By the time other skiers and rescuers got to him, he had already passed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Another avalanche fatality occurred on January 27th, this one in the Meadows area of Big Cottonwood Canyon.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A skier triggered an avalanche in an area known as Meadows, a backcountry area in Big Cottonwood Canyon, out-of-bounds of nearby Solitude Ski Resort. The avalanche buried the victim; he was found and dug out less than an hour after the avalanche occurred, but had already passed away.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A snowmobiler was caught and killed in an avalanche on Grandview Peak, in the Session Mountains, east of Bountiful, Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A man was snowmobiling with some friends on Grandview Peak, east of the city of Bountiful, when he and another man triggered an avalanche. The other man was able to outrun the avalanche on his snowmobile, but the victim was not, and he was buried under about 6 feet of snow. He was buried for approximately 15-18 minutes, before being completely dug out, and was flown to University Hospital in Salt Lake in extremely critical condition. He passed away two days later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and very moist Pacific storm system brought widespread heavy precipitation to Utah over a five day period. The storm pulled a continuous stream of tropical moisture ahead of it and, combined with numerous embedded shortwaves, resulted in almost non-stop rainfall across many of the lower elevations and wet, heavy snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Storm totals for the 5-day event included 84 inches at Brighton Crest, 76 inches at Deer Valley Daly West, 73 inches at Sundance - Mid Mnt., 70 inches at Alta Ski Lifts, 70 inches at Provo Canyon, 54 inches at Solitude Mountain Resort, and 50 inches at Snowbird Ski and Summer Resort.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and very moist Pacific storm system brought widespread heavy precipitation to Utah over a five day period. The storm pulled a continuous stream of tropical moisture ahead of it and, combined with numerous embedded shortwaves, resulted in almost non-stop rainfall across many of the lower elevations and wet, heavy snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: While flooding in Kane County was less than in nearby Washington County, significant damage still occurred. Many roads were closed or impassable due to flood waters, including Highway 14, the 3rd South Connector in Kanab, and US-89 east of Kanab, the last of which had 12 inches of water flowing over the road. A landslide also occurred on US-89 near milepost 93, where debris was approximately 4 feet deep and 50-60 feet wide. Residential flooding occurred in Orderville and Glendale, and many private roads in those two towns were washed out.

Strong winds were felt across much of the state on the 30th ahead of the storm, with large amounts of blowing dust reported in some areas. The storm finally entered the state on the 31st, bringing widespread heavy snow, particularly in the higher terrain. Note that this episode continued into April.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A maximum wind gust of 58 mph was recorded in South Ogden.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and strongest in a series of winter storms brought widespread snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado, as well as rain and snow to the lower valleys. The adverse weather conditions caused the closure of highway 191 from Monticello to Peter's Hill.  Several schools and businesses were also closed during this storm.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and strongest in a series of winter storms brought widespread snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado, as well as rain and snow to the lower valleys. Strong south to southwest winds generated blizzard conditions over the southern mountains. Lower elevations of southeast Utah experienced a mix of rain and snow on top of the earlier week's snowpack, which lead to some localized flooding problems near the town of Bluff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 12 to 36 inches occurred, with locally higher amounts of up to 43 inches measured at the Camp Jackson SNOTEL site. The weight of the heavy snow, along with the week's earlier snowfall, caused the collapse of several garage and shed roofs. Widespread blizzard conditions occurred throughout the first several hours of the storm, and thereafter, wind gusts up to 40 mph continued to produce areas of blowing and drifting snow. The adverse weather caused school and business closures across San Juan county.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The third and strongest in a series of winter storms brought widespread snowfall across the mountains of eastern Utah and western Colorado, as well as rain and snow to the lower valleys. Strong south to southwest winds generated blizzard conditions over the southern mountains. Lower elevations of southeast Utah experienced a mix of rain and snow on top of the earlier week's snowpack, which lead to some localized flooding problems near the town of Bluff.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of about 3 to 6 inches occurred above the 5,000 foot level. Locally higher amounts included 10 inches in Blanding and 8 inches in the Valley of the Gods area. Adverse weather conditions caused school and business closures across San Juan County. Lower elevations below 5,000 feet experienced a mix of rain and snow with this storm, which fell on top of the week's previous snowfall. This caused some localized flooding of lower lying buildings and leaking roofs in and around the town of Bluff.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large and very moist Pacific storm system brought widespread heavy precipitation to Utah over a five day period. The storm pulled a continuous stream of tropical moisture ahead of it and, combined with numerous embedded shortwaves, resulted in almost non-stop rainfall across many of the lower elevations and wet, heavy snow in the mountains.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused widespread significant flooding across Washington County. Rainfall and significantly increased river flows began on December 19th, with many rivers and streams around Zion National Park and St. George near bankfull. Reports of road flooding began on December 20th. SR-9 was closed in Zion National Park due to flooding between the tunnel and Mt. Carmel Junction, and flooding and rockslides occurred on SR-18 between Enterprise and Central.||The worst of the flooding occurred on December 21st, when the flow of the Santa Clara River increased to an estimated 5000 cfs, and the Virgin River near Bloomington was measured at 12400 cfs. Zion National Park was closed due to flooding, and the Zion Park Lodge was evacuated due to the danger of landslides. One historic landslide, in particular, was moving through the day as the riprap structure was eroded away. Old Highway 91 was also closed, as flooding and debris washed out and damaged much of the road. Many other roadways across the county were flooded, including many roads in the city of St. George, many rural roads along the Santa Clara, SR-18 south of Central, in Enterprise along Center Street and Main Street, and the road into Snow Canyon State Park, which closed the park to tourists. Many bridges were damaged or destroyed, including the foot bridge over the Fort Pearce Wash, the Tobin Wash Bridge near Gunlock, a foot bridge at the Sunbrook Golf Course, the bridge at 2500 South in New Harmony, and the bridge at Brookside. Residential flood damage was reported in Enterprise, Springdale, Rockville, and St. George. Contaminated water became a problem along the Virgin River, and culinary water became unavailable in the town of Gunlock. The Southgate Golf Course in St. George suffered extensive damage due to erosion.||Flooding continued on December 22nd. Flood waters damaged a bridge in New Harmony Heights, closed many roadways including a bridge near the Enterprise Reservoir, and up to 15 homes were damaged to varying degrees in Brookside. River flows stayed high on December 23rd before receding.||Damage estimates were obtained from the Utah Emergency Management Association, and included damages of $4.7 million to infrastructure and $15 million to other properties. In addition, $300,000 worth of heavy construction equipment was saved when the NWS forecast office in Salt Lake City called the Washington City Public Works, notifying of them of the upcoming high flows expected on the Virgin River. The Public Works pulled all of their equipment out of the Virgin River, which they said would have been swept away and lost if not for the phone call.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A major winter storm took a three day period to move through northern Utah, bringing heavy wet snow to the area. Though the snowfall remained primarily in northern Utah, southern Utah received strong gusty winds as this storm moved through the region. The combination of snowfall and strong winds in northern Utah also produced hazardous avalanche conditions, with one fatal avalanche during this period of time.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: One snowmobiler was killed in an avalanche that he triggered on Sunday, April 4th. He was traveling north along a ridge, when a cornice he was on, cracked and broke off. This caused an avalanche to release below the cornice. He was carried over a cliff band. He was buried approximately 1,000 feet below the ridge under 2 to 3 feet of debris. He was located by his partner with a beacon, and was taken away from the scene via helicopter. However, the helicopter medical crew could not find a pulse, and the man was pronounced dead an hour later.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed ahead of an approaching cold front, producing widespread areas of blowing dust, with damage primarily reported in northern Utah.

The major damage in the area occurred near the National Weather Service radar at Promontory Point, where 21 power poles that supply power to the radar were snapped.

Trees were uprooted in Sandy, tree limbs were broken and shingles were blown off some roofs in Tooele, and a semitrailer truck was knocked over along I-80 just east of Saltair.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 12 to 20 inches occurred.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The southern and central mountains were favored, as well as the southern Colorado and southeast Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall occurred across about half of the area with amounts ranging from 5 to 7 inches. Some areas saw lesser amounts, including the town of Moab with only about 2 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The southern and central mountains were favored, as well as the southern Colorado and southeast Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 5 to 7 inches occurred above the 5,500 foot level, with about 2 inches or less below. Wind gusts up to 35 mph produced areas of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Dense fog formed under a strong surface-based inversion.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Areas of dense fog occurred across the area with visibilities down to a quarter mile or less.

CZ_NAME: GREAT SALT LAKE DESERT AND MOUNTAINS
EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Strong and gusty southerly winds developed ahead of an approaching cold front on June 16th, with severe gusts reported across much of the state. In addition, winds remained very gusty behind the cold front in some locations.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: High wind reports across the Great Salt Lake Desert and Mountains included 65 mph at Camel Back Mountain, 64 mph at Lakeside Mountain, 63 mph at Promontory Point, 62 mph at the Causeway sensor in the Dugway Proving Ground mesonet, and 58 mph at Hat Island.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally warm temperatures in early June, combined with a deep late season snowpack, led to excessive snowmelt across northern Utah. This caused flooding on both Little Cottonwood Creek and the Weber River near Oakley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Little Cottonwood Creek flooded for multiple days, with the gauge located on Little Cottonwood Creek at Crestwood Park peaking at 891 cfs on June 7th. Damage was reported in homes, apartments and businesses in Sandy, Cottonwood Heights, Murray and Midvale. Many of these buildings experienced flooding in backyards, basements, patios, and parking areas. Some damage in these areas was avoided due to an extensive sandbagging effort.||Some of the most extensive flood damage occurred in areas of Murray Park, State Street near the park, and surrounding structures to the park, with water as deep as 4 to 5 feet reported in parts of Murray Park. It was in this area that the street flooding was most widespread; multiple lanes of traffic on State Street were closed due to flooding, with water as much as a foot deep in some spots. Flooding also occurred on Vine Street, one block west of State Street.  ||Damage also occurred in Little Cottonwood Canyon, where the creek had cut 20 to 30 feet into the mountain below the road near the mouth of the canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abnormally warm temperatures in early June, combined with a deep late season snowpack, led to excessive snowmelt across northern Utah. This caused flooding on both Little Cottonwood Creek and the Weber River near Oakley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding was reported along the Weber River from Oakley to Hoytsville. The gauge on the Weber River near Oakley exceeded the flood stage of 4.5 feet on June 6th, with flooding continuing into June 10th. The gauge reached a peak of 3179 cfs on the evening of June 8th. ||Damage was reported in the communities of Oakley and Peoa, with water going over the top of some bridges and ending up in homes. Many homes and summer cabins were damaged, especially in basements and yards. Much of the damage occurred to structures at the mouth of the upper Weber River, and in homes along State Route 21 in Oakley. Residents used sandbags in several areas to try to control some of the flooding. Flood waters also ran over a portion of State Route 32, causing the road to close.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts across the state of Utah, with damage reported in the southern Utah town of Hurricane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The sensor at the Utah Test and Training Range Thermal Treatment Unit recorded a wind gust of 58 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An upper level low pressure system pushed a cold front from north to south across the region, and was followed by a Pacific stream of moisture that brought prolonged snowfall to portions of eastern Utah and western Colorado. The southern and central mountains were favored, as well as the southern Colorado and southeast Utah valleys.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall of 5 to 8 inches occurred with wind gusts of 30 mph producing areas of blowing snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Clearing skies moved over Eastern Utah behind a cold frontal passage which resulted in unseasonably cool temperatures across the eastern Uintah Basin.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Minimum temperatures across the area ranged from 25 to 30 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms spread precipitation across southeast Utah throughout the month of March, improving drought conditions from abnormally dry and moderate drought to near normal by the end of the month, while northeast Utah remained abnormally dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions persisted across the area, except in the extreme northeast portion of the area where moderate drought conditions persisted throughout the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms spread precipitation across southeast Utah throughout the month of March, improving drought conditions from abnormally dry and moderate drought to near normal by the end of the month, while northeast Utah remained abnormally dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions across the entire area at the beginning of the month persisted across northern parts of the area throughout the month, while southern portions improved to no drought conditions by the end of the month.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms spread precipitation across southeast Utah throughout the month of March, improving drought conditions from abnormally dry and moderate drought to near normal by the end of the month, while northeast Utah remained abnormally dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions at the beginning of the month improved by the end of the month to a situation with no more drought.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of winter storms spread precipitation across southeast Utah throughout the month of March, improving drought conditions from abnormally dry and moderate drought to near normal by the end of the month, while northeast Utah remained abnormally dry.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Significant improvement in drought conditions occurred throughout the month. Abnormally dry conditions in the northern area and moderate drought conditions in the southern area improved by the end of the month to a situation with no more drought across the entire area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Very strong winds developed ahead of an approaching cold front, producing widespread areas of blowing dust, with damage primarily reported in northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The U.S. 6 at Eureka sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 88 mph, while the Fish Springs sensor recorded a gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A windy storm system entered Utah on May 27th, bringing scattered rain showers and strong gusty winds. These winds were strong enough to cause damage in the Sevier Valley.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Richfield Airport AWOS recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph, with winds greater than 50 mph for 3 hours. Damage near Richfield included an awning that was ripped off of a greenhouse. In another part of town, the arches fell off of a sign for a McDonald's restaurant, landing on a car in the parking lot. That car was totaled, while two other nearby cars had minor damage. In addition, a 14 year old who was in one of the cars at the time, was injured.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A couple of thunderstorms produced severe wind gusts across the state of Utah, with damage reported in the southern Utah town of Hurricane.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms in Hurricane, Utah damaged two twenty year old trees, with an ash tree being snapped and a plum tree being uprooted. In addition, a building along the street of 600 North received roof damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: As low pressure intensified over western Utah, easterly canyon winds developed along the Wasatch Front, with severe winds and some property damage reported in the northern Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A wind gust of 60 mph was recorded in South Ogden. In addition, some trees and power poles were snapped in Farmington.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked through northeast Utah and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1 inch in diameter was reported.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A severe thunderstorm tracked through northeast Utah and produced large hail.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail up to 1.25 inches in diameter pummelled the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah, leading to more flash flooding in southern Utah, as well as an EF0 tornado near Wellington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A tornado touched down near Wellington, with multiple members of the public seeing the tornado and taking pictures. The tornado damaged a trailer, rolling it over onto its side, and uprooted a few large trees.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Subtropical moisture produced heavy rainfall to the north of Arches National Park during the night of August 16 into August 17th.  Resultant runoff produced flash flooding downstream within Arches National Park during the afternoon of August 17th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from the previous night well north of Arches National Park resulted in flash flooding in the afternoon and early evening within the Salt Wash drainage that runs though Arches National Park.  Flood waters over a foot deep were flowing over the NPS road in the vicinity of Wolfe Ranch which leads to Delicate Arch.  The road was closed to traffic for at least 7 hours.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was in place at the end of July, remained in place into August, with widespread afternoon thunderstorms across Utah. While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain over the Lake Fork burn scar caused a flash flood and debris flow, with Highway 89 being covered in water, mud, and rocks near Birdseye. The flood closed the highway in both directions between mile markers 305 and 307, and some cars were stuck in the debris.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was in place at the end of July, remained in place into August, with widespread afternoon thunderstorms across Utah. While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding along the Scenic Drive of Capitol Reef National Park, including the Fremont River, Sulphur Creek, the Grand Wash, and Capitol Gorge. People stranded in the Grand Wash had to be rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large portion of Uintah county in northeast Utah improved to near normal conditions, while extreme southeastern portions of the state worsened to abnormally dry and moderate drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions spread over southern portions of the area, while moderate drought conditions developed across the extreme southern portion of San Juan County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions spread over the whole area with moderate drought across the extreme southern portion of San Juan County.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Most of the area improved to near normal conditions with only a small eastern portion of the area that remained abnormally dry.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A large portion of Uintah county in northeast Utah improved to near normal conditions, while extreme southeastern portions of the state worsened to abnormally dry and moderate drought.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Central and western portions of the area improved to near normal conditions.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions across eastern Utah were unchanged through the month, except for a sliver of southern San Juan County that improved to abnormally dry conditions.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The extreme southern San Juan County drought status improved from moderate drought to abnormally dry conditions due to an active monsoon period.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity was relentless through the end of July, with more flash flooding, primarily in southern Utah, as well as more widespread severe thunderstorms, in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain fell on the Mill Flat burn scar near New Harmony, causing a debris flow and flash flooding in New Harmony. The flooding emanated primarily from Pinto Creek, and to a lesser extent, from Main, Dam, and Straight Canyons.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity was relentless through the end of July, with more flash flooding, primarily in southern Utah, as well as more widespread severe thunderstorms, in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Bottom, Gate, and Cottonwood Canyons.  Bulldozers had to clean off nearby roads to open them for business, and some tourists had to be rescued.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity was relentless through the end of July, with more flash flooding, primarily in southern Utah, as well as more widespread severe thunderstorms, in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding caused roads to be damaged and washed out in Croton and Last Chance Canyons. Park rangers could not access these canyons due to the road damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred at the headwaters of several creeks at the north end of Capitol Reef National Park, with the Fremont River gauge at Caineville rising from 58 cfs to 700 cfs in one hour.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused the Paria River to increase from 20 cfs to 4000 cfs. Travel time on the flood was a little more than 6 hours, with the majority of the rain falling just southeast of Bryce Canyon National Park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An unusually moist airmass across the area resulted in heavy rain producing thunderstorms over eastern Utah where saturated soils already existed, resulting in areas of flash flooding.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A normally dry wash behind the Arches National Park Visitor Center quickly filled with runoff from heavy rain and was running 4 to 5 feet deep and 4 to 5 feet wide. Significant amounts of runoff also flowed through Courthouse Wash just west of the visitor's center.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding across the northern portions of Capitol Reef National Park, including Cathedral Valley, further damaging roads that were already in disrepair from previous flash flooding. In addition, several washes east of the park were flooded, and water was flowing over SR-24.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple roads at the southern end of Capitol Reef National Park were flooded and damaged, most notably the Notom-Bullfrog road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved through Utah near the end of August, with gusty synoptic scale winds and more flash flooding in southern Utah from associated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in Coyote Valley and Buckskin Gulch was reported by BLM backcountry rangers. Logs were removed that were 10 feet overhead in the slots, and new pools of water had formed. In this event, the Buckskin Gulch flooding was primarily due to rain that fell over Coyote Valley.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A slow moving thunderstorm produced very heavy rain, and caused flash flooding along SR-24 between Loa and Koosharem. There were several accidents on the highway as the storm was overhead. Note that this storm also produced severe hail.

Several accidents were reported on the highway as the storm was overhead. Note that this storm also caused flash flooding in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Afternoon thunderstorms produced widespread strong winds across northern Utah. These winds caused damage near Salt Lake City, as well as along the Legacy Highway in Farmington and Kaysville. The associated cold front that moved through Utah behind these storms also caused strong post-frontal winds, particularly in the west desert and in the southern Wasatch Front.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms moved through the Salt Lake Valley. Recorded wind gusts included 76 mph at the Salt Lake City International Airport FAA sensor, 65 mph at the Salt Lake City International Airport ASOS, and 64 mph at the Baccus/SR-111 sensor. Damage from this thunderstorm included large trees knocked down in the Avenues neighborhood of Salt Lake City, power poles knocked down along I-80 west of the Salt Lake City International Airport, and damage to the Airport fences, hangers, and signs.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: A strong line of thunderstorms moved through the northern Wasatch Front, with maximum recorded wind gusts of 64 mph at the Bountiful KSL sensor and 59 mph at the Centerville UDOT sensor. Power poles were knocked down along the Legacy Highway, causing a closure while crews repaired the damage.

EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain north of Koosharem caused a mudslide that blocked SR-62 near milepost 40. All lanes along the highway were closed until the slide could be cleaned up.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active monsoon season continued into the second week of August, with thunderstorms causing more flash flooding, large hail, and strong winds. The monsoon moisture that had been over Utah for nearly 3 weeks finally moved off to the east on August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain at the headwaters of the Fort Pearce Wash, with an estimated total of over 3.5 inches of precipitation, caused flash flooding along the wash. The travel time to St. George was  over 6 hours. The Fort Pearce gauge rose from zero to 680 cfs. The golf course attendant at St. George indicated the flood was the greatest he had seen in 6 years.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Drought conditions improved over portions of northeast Utah during November, but remained abnormally dry across the extreme northeast corner of the state.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions remained in place across extreme Northeast Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The active monsoon season continued into the second week of August, with thunderstorms causing more flash flooding, large hail, and strong winds. The monsoon moisture that had been over Utah for nearly 3 weeks finally moved off to the east on August 8th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Hat Island sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 71 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused a debris flow from the south end of the Corner Canyon burn scar, damaging about 20 homes with mud and debris. Highland Drive was closed, and the police station, courts, and city hall were all flooded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A Pacific storm system moved through Utah near the end of August, with gusty synoptic scale winds and more flash flooding in southern Utah from associated thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in portions of Zion National Park and the surrounding area, including in North Creek, Deep Creek, and the North Fork of the Virgin River. An increase in flow of over 500 cfs was measured at the Virgin River gauge.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of extreme northeast Utah remained abnormally dry, while improvement to near normal conditions spread into extreme southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions remained in place over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of extreme northeast Utah remained abnormally dry, while improvement to near normal conditions spread into extreme southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions remained in place over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of extreme northeast Utah remained abnormally dry, while improvement to near normal conditions spread into extreme southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions remained in place over the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Portions of extreme northeast Utah remained abnormally dry, while improvement to near normal conditions spread into extreme southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Abnormally dry conditions were alleviated by the end of the month across extreme southeast Utah.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Runoff from thunderstorms caused minor flooding in areas of Arches National Park.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall caused minor flooding of small streams and roads within Arches National Park, as well as other low lying areas.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoonal moisture combined with daytime heating to generate heavy rain producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain producing thunderstorms caused water from Deep Creek to flow over a 70 foot section of Deep Creek Road near Lapoint.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant moisture across the area and daytime heating resulted in the development of strong afternoon thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: At least 6 inches of water and debris rushed across Highway 211 between the junction of Highway 191 and Newspaper Rock. Also, pea-sized hailstones accumulated in some areas up to 6 inches deep.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Gusty south winds, along with very dry conditions, developed ahead of an approaching cold front. When a wildfire was started at Camp Williams by machine gun fire, the winds helped the fire to rapidly spread into the neighboring community of Herriman, destroying multiple homes and forcing an evacuation of the neighborhood.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Utah National Guard took target practice on their machine gun firing range at Camp Williams on the afternoon of September 19th. Shrapnel from this exercise ignited brush around the area and, with the help of wind gusts generally in the 30-40 mph range and relative humidity values between 5 and 10 percent, the fire quickly spread. This wildfire eventually threatened the nearby town of Herriman, specifically the subdivisions of The Cove at Herriman Springs and the High Country Estates II, resulting in the mandatory evacuation of an estimated 5,000 people in over 1,600 homes. By the time the fire was stopped and contained, it had burned over 4,300 acres, completely destroying 3 houses and damaging at least 400 other houses. Several people were injured due to the fire, primarily police officers and firefighters responding to the blaze, with the most common injury being smoke inhalation. The listed end time is when the fire was 100% contained.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was in place at the end of July, remained in place into August, with widespread afternoon thunderstorms across Utah. While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in the Cathedral District on the north end of Capitol Reef National Park. The heavy rain made roads impassable and virtually destroyed them, leaving much of the area impassable until county road crews were able to rebuild the roads.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was in place at the end of July, remained in place into August, with widespread afternoon thunderstorms across Utah. While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Price River gauge rose from 50 cfs to 480 cfs. Widespread rainfall amounts of more than 2 inches in 3 hours were observed in the area.

While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: State park rangers reported that heavy rains washed out roads in multiple locations north of Goblin Valley State Park.

While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail measured to be one inch in diameter was reported in Beaver.

While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Penny-sized hail, or hail of approximately 0.75 inches in diameter, was reported near Sandy.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Multiple slot canyons were flooded on the western edge of Glen Canyon National Recreation Area, including Coyote Gulch. Roads were impassable near Coyote Gulch and Sunset Flats, and Hole-in-the-Rock road sustained damage.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Twenty-five Mile Wash. In addition, roads were impassable in the surrounding area, including damage to Hole-in-the-Rock road, further north than the road was damaged earlier in the day.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Buckskin Gulch, with water flowing over nearby US-89.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After 1.5 to 2.0 inches of rain fell near Parowan, flash flooding occurred on SR-143, with two feet of water and debris crossing the road.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a nearly stationary upper level low over California, large amounts of moisture spread into Utah. This allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the state, with severe thunderstorms developing primarily across southern and eastern Utah, and widespread flooding occurring across southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Water and flood debris flowed across the Scenic Drive in Capitol Reef National Park, closing the road to all tourists. In addition, the Dirty Devil River, which normally flows at 10 to 20 cfs, reached a peak of 6300 cfs on October 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a nearly stationary upper level low over California, large amounts of moisture spread into Utah. This allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the state, with severe thunderstorms developing primarily across southern and eastern Utah, and widespread flooding occurring across southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flooding occurred across many drainages in Garfield County, including Calf Creek and the Escalante River. The Escalante River, which typically flows below 10 cfs, reached a peak of 1300 cfs on October 5th. In addition, the Dirty Devil River, which normally flows at 10 to 20 cfs, reached a peak of 6300 cfs on October 6th.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold and moist storms moved through Utah in the last week of October. These storms dropped the first significant snowfall of the season on the higher terrain across the state, and also caused strong gusty winds in valleys across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first storm of the season, 1.00 to 2.00 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation fell across the central mountains of Utah, with much of that falling as snow over the higher peaks.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a nearly stationary upper level low over California, large amounts of moisture spread into Utah. This allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the state, with severe thunderstorms developing primarily across southern and eastern Utah, and widespread flooding occurring across southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: An EF0 tornado touched down west of Canyonlands National Park, at the south end of Robbers Roost Flat. Approximately 30 juniper trees were either snapped or uprooted by the tornado. No monetary damage occurred as the tornado occurred in a rural area and no trees fell on the nearby dirt road, so no cleanup was necessary.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold and moist storms moved through Utah in the last week of October. These storms dropped the first significant snowfall of the season on the higher terrain across the state, and also caused strong gusty winds in valleys across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first storm of the season, more than a foot of snow fell on the highest elevations of the northern Wasatch Mountains. Liquid equivalent precipitation totals of 2.00 to 4.00 inches were common across the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold and moist storms moved through Utah in the last week of October. These storms dropped the first significant snowfall of the season on the higher terrain across the state, and also caused strong gusty winds in valleys across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: In the first storm of the season, heavy snow fell over the Uinta Mountains. The area generally received 1.00 to 2.00 inches of liquid equivalent precipitation, almost all of which fell as snow at the highest elevations.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold and moist storms moved through Utah in the last week of October. These storms dropped the first significant snowfall of the season on the higher terrain across the state, and also caused strong gusty winds in valleys across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Alta Ski Lifts received 20 inches of new snow for the event, while higher elevations, above the snow level for a longer period of time, received much more. Liquid equivalent precipitation totals across the area were generally between 2.00 and 4.00 inches.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A series of cold and moist storms moved through Utah in the last week of October. These storms dropped the first significant snowfall of the season on the higher terrain across the state, and also caused strong gusty winds in valleys across northern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Maximum recorded wind gusts included 60 mph at The University of Utah, 59 mph at the Pony Express Marker sensor, and 58 mph at the Lake Point I-80 sensor.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a nearly stationary upper level low over California, large amounts of moisture spread into Utah. This allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the state, with severe thunderstorms developing primarily across southern and eastern Utah, and widespread flooding occurring across southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in Capitol Reef National Park, including along SR-24 and in the Cathedral areas of the park.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After heavy rains passed over the area, park rangers reported water flowing along State Route 24, as well as flash flooding on the Scenic Drive, the Grand Wash, and a few other washes in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: After an estimated rainfall of about 4 inches in 2.5 hours, the Paria River flooded basements in the city of Tropic, and flowed over small bridges leading to nearby residential areas. The flood wave continued down the Paria all the way down to the Utah/Arizona border.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding on Highway 6 near Woodside. Water was running across one lane of the highway, forcing local law enforcement to reduce traffic flow down to one lane until the waters receded.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred along Bullfrog Creek. This flooding damaged a dirt road that crosses the creek near its confluence with Lake Powell.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm winds caused damage in Neola, knocking over several trees and a small barn.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding was reported along Calf Creek and in surrounding drainages at the headwaters of the Escalante River.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoon moisture moved into southern and central Utah in mid-July, allowing thunderstorms to become more widespread each afternoon. This moisture, combined with the fact that many of these thunderstorms were slow moving, led to flash flooding in many locations across southern Utah. Further north, storms were a little faster moving, and isolated thunderstorm wind damage was reported.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Sheets Gulch, on the eastern edge of Capitol Reef National Park. Water also flowed over the Notom Road, forcing rangers to close the road. People in the area, who observed the flash flooding, described it as a class 4 rapid coming out of the slot canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah, leading to more flash flooding in southern Utah, as well as an EF0 tornado near Wellington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in multiple slot canyons and high travel areas in Zion National Park. These areas included Lodge Canyon, Pine Creek Canyon, Spry Canyon, Clear Creek, and Highway 9. Three hikers were caught in the Spry Canyon flood and went over two cliffs, the first of which was 40 feet high, and the second 60 feet high. All three men survived the ordeal.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorms continued across Utah, leading to more flash flooding in southern Utah, as well as an EF0 tornado near Wellington.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding on US-89 in Marysville Canyon, near Big Rock Candy Mountain. Mud flowed across the road, blocking traffic and causing several vehicles to get stuck.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Thunderstorm activity was relentless through the end of July, with more flash flooding, primarily in southern Utah, as well as more widespread severe thunderstorms, in both northern and southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding in Zion National Park occurred in Keyhole Canyon and Pine Creek Canyon, a technical slot canyon.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A low pressure storm system moved from western Utah into south-central New Mexico spreading rain and snow across much of eastern Utah. Heavy snow developed over the eastern Utah mountains, with significant snow accumulations in a few lower elevation areas of southeast Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy snowfall produced accumulations of about 12 to 13 inches above the 8,000 foot level, while accumulations below ranged from 4 to 12 inches.  Wind gusts to 30 mph produced areas of blowing and drifting snow.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall, as well as hail and outflow winds that approached severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall from an area of intense thunderstorms produced flash flood waters that crossed Highway 128 at a number of locations between mile markers 8 and 14.  Debris consisting mostly of mud up to two feet deep was deposited on the Highway.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Monsoonal moisture resulted in strong thunderstorms which produced heavy rainfall, as well as hail and outflow winds that approached severe criteria.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rainfall within a short period of time resulted in flash flooding across the heavily used Mineral Bottom Road.  Mineral Bottom Road was deeply washed out in the switchback section and an extensive amount of debris covered the road, which remained impassable as of mid September 2010.  The road washout stranded 29 people who were airlifted by helicopter.  The KGJX WSR-88D radar estimated 1.86 inches of rain within 60 minutes in the source region of the Mineral Bottom Road flash flood. The NPS' Island-In-The-Sky visitors center personnel measured 1.05 inches of rain, most of which fell within a half hour. Other locations in the area likely received flash flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A strong Pacific storm moved slowly across the area on April 21st, initiating many thunderstorms across northern Utah, with some of them being severe.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Hail with a diameter of 0.75 inches and 1.00 inch were both reported with this storm near Lehi.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Deep moisture returned to Utah on August 16th, picking up where it left off earlier in the month by causing widespread flash flooding and strong gusty winds.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Flash flooding occurred in numerous slot canyons in the Glen Canyon National Recreation Area, including Moody Wash, East Moody Canyon, and Middle Moody Canyon. In addition, multiple roads were made impassable due to the flooding.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: High-based dry thunderstorms brought strong microburst winds to parts of northern Utah on September 14th.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: The Gunnison Island sensor recorded a maximum wind gust of 60 mph.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: The moist airmass that was in place at the end of July, remained in place into August, with widespread afternoon thunderstorms across Utah. While flash flooding and severe thunderstorms were reported in both northern and southern Utah, the flooding primarily occurred across the southern half of the State, whereas large hail and gusty winds were more common in northern Utah, where there was a better shear environment.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused flash flooding in Alvey Wash, North Creek, and many other washes in the area.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Abundant monsoonal moisture combined with daytime heating to generate heavy rain producing thunderstorms.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Heavy rain caused one foot of water to flow over a bike path along side Mill Creek Road in Moab.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: With a nearly stationary upper level low over California, large amounts of moisture spread into Utah. This allowed numerous showers and thunderstorms to develop across the state, with severe thunderstorms developing primarily across southern and eastern Utah, and widespread flooding occurring across southern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Two 17 year old males were struck by lightning just outside Snow Canyon High School in St. George. The two boys were waiting for a ride home shortly after school let out; they took shelter from the rain under a nearby tree, where lightning struck and injured them both. CPR was performed at the site, and they were both in critical condition when they reached a hospital in Las Vegas, but both survived and are expected to make a full recovery.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: Unseasonably cold temperatures followed a cold front passage.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Temperatures dropped during the night, with reported minimums throughout the eastern Uintah Basin ranging from 27 to 32 degrees Fahrenheit.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: A late season winter storm brought accumulating snowfall the higher elevations of eastern Utah and western Colorado, including many of the highly traveled mountain passes.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall amounts across the area ranged from 5 to 7 inches above the 9,000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved through the Rocky Mountain region spreading a round of late season snow across the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 4 to 7 inches occurred mainly above the 8,000 foot level.

EPISODE_NARRATIVE: An area of low pressure moved through the Rocky Mountain region spreading a round of late season snow across the mountains of eastern Utah.
EVENT_NARRATIVE: Snowfall of 5 to 6 inches occurred mainly above the 8,000 foot level. Wind gusts up to 35 mph generated areas of blowing snow.
